SGP Model Results — Moonlight Graham League

What the data says about your league’s category economics

Published

March 24, 2026

What is an SGP Denominator?

Each SGP denominator answers one question: how many units of a stat does it take to gain one standings point in the Moonlight Graham league?

For example, an HR denominator of 12.2 means that a team accumulating 12 more home runs than the team below it in the standings will, on average, finish one place higher in the HR category.

These denominators are the conversion rate between raw stats and standings value. They are calibrated from 6 years of actual league standings (2019, 2021-2025), and they are specific to this league — not generic AL-only values.

Show code 
counting <- DENOMS |>
  filter(!category %in% c("AVG", "ERA", "WHIP")) |>
  mutate(category = fct_reorder(category, denom))

rate <- DENOMS |>
  filter(category %in% c("AVG", "ERA", "WHIP")) |>
  mutate(category = fct_reorder(category, denom))

p_count <- ggplot(counting, aes(x = denom, y = category, color = side)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = denom, yend = category), linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", denom)),
    hjust = -0.4,
    size = 3.5,
    color = pal$mid,
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(Batting = pal$bat, Pitching = pal$pit)) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.18))) +
  labs(title = "Counting stats", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  panel_title +
  h_grid

p_rate <- ggplot(rate, aes(x = denom, y = category, color = side)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = denom, yend = category), linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5) +
  geom_text(
    aes(
      label = case_when(
        denom < 0.01 ~ sprintf("%.5f", denom),
        denom < 0.1 ~ sprintf("%.4f", denom),
        TRUE ~ sprintf("%.3f", denom)
      )
    ),
    hjust = -0.3,
    size = 3.5,
    color = pal$mid,
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(Batting = pal$bat, Pitching = pal$pit)) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.25))) +
  labs(
    title = "Rate stats",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    caption = "Blue = batting \u00b7 Red = pitching"
  ) +
  panel_title +
  h_grid

p_count | p_rate + plot_layout(widths = c(3, 2))

Reading the denominators

Category Denominator Plain English
R 31.8 32 more runs = +1 standings point
HR 12.2 12 more HR = +1 standings point
RBI 30.9 31 more RBI = +1 standings point
SB 10.4 10 more SB = +1 standings point
AVG .0031 3 points of team batting average = +1 standings point
W 3.0 3 more wins = +1 standings point
SV 6.4 6-7 more saves = +1 standings point
SO 35.7 36 more strikeouts = +1 standings point
ERA 0.101 0.10 lower team ERA = +1 standings point
WHIP 0.019 0.019 lower team WHIP = +1 standings point

Key takeaway: Wins are the tightest pitching category — just 3 wins separate standings places. This makes wins the most leveraged category in the league: a single good starter acquisition can swing 2-3 standings points.

The Spending Split: Your League is Shifting

Show code 
active <- rosters |>
  filter(status == "act") |>
  mutate(is_pitcher = position == "P")

splits_df <- active |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(
    hitter_dollars = sum(salary[!is_pitcher], na.rm = TRUE),
    pitcher_dollars = sum(salary[is_pitcher], na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    total = hitter_dollars + pitcher_dollars,
    hitter_pct = hitter_dollars / total * 100,
    pitcher_pct = pitcher_dollars / total * 100
  )

last_row <- splits_df |> slice_max(year)

p_pct <- ggplot(splits_df, aes(x = year)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    aes(ymin = hitter_pct, ymax = 100),
    fill = pal$pit,
    alpha = 0.08
  ) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = 0, ymax = hitter_pct), fill = pal$bat, alpha = 0.08) +
  geom_line(aes(y = hitter_pct), color = pal$bat, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(y = hitter_pct), color = pal$bat, size = 2.5) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 65,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = pal$light,
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = min(splits_df$year) + 0.1,
    y = 66.5,
    label = "Model optimal (65%)",
    color = pal$light,
    size = 3,
    hjust = 0
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = last_row$year + 0.2,
    y = last_row$hitter_pct - 3,
    label = paste0(round(last_row$hitter_pct), "% hitters"),
    color = pal$bat,
    size = 3.5,
    fontface = "bold",
    hjust = 0
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = last_row$year + 0.2,
    y = last_row$hitter_pct + last_row$pitcher_pct / 2,
    label = paste0(round(last_row$pitcher_pct), "% pitchers"),
    color = pal$pit,
    size = 3.5,
    fontface = "bold",
    hjust = 0
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(40, 100), labels = \(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = splits_df$year) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  labs(title = "Spending split over time", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  panel_title

splits_long <- splits_df |>
  pivot_longer(
    c(hitter_dollars, pitcher_dollars),
    names_to = "type",
    values_to = "dollars"
  ) |>
  mutate(type = ifelse(type == "hitter_dollars", "Hitters", "Pitchers"))

p_dollars <- ggplot(splits_long, aes(x = year, y = dollars, fill = type)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(Hitters = pal$bat, Pitchers = pal$pit),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(prefix = "$", big.mark = ",")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = splits_df$year) +
  labs(title = "Raw dollar amounts", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  panel_title +
  theme(
    legend.position = c(0.82, 0.92),
    legend.direction = "horizontal",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.key.size = unit(0.35, "cm"),
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = alpha(pal$bg, 0.8), color = NA)
  )

p_pct | p_dollars

The league has been steadily shifting money from hitters to pitchers:

  • 2019: 67% hitters / 33% pitchers
  • 2025: 59% hitters / 41% pitchers

That’s an 8-point swing over 6 seasons. The model says the “correct” split based on the SGP structure is roughly 65/35. If this trend continues, pitchers may become overvalued at auction — which would make hitters relative bargains.

Draft Day Implication

If the league continues spending ~59% on hitters, you may find more value targeting hitting. The market is pricing pitching higher than the model’s SGP-based valuation suggests.

Category Competitiveness

Not all categories are created equal. Some are bunched tight (hard to gain ground), others are spread wide (high upside, but also high downside).

Show code 
cv_data <- map_dfr(c(BATTING, PITCHING), function(cat) {
  if (cat %in% c("R", "SO")) {
    vals <- primary |> filter(year >= 2019) |> pull(!!sym(cat)) |> na.omit()
  } else {
    vals <- primary |> pull(!!sym(cat)) |> na.omit()
  }
  tibble(
    category = cat,
    cv = sd(vals) / mean(vals),
    side = ifelse(cat %in% BATTING, "Batting", "Pitching")
  )
}) |>
  mutate(category = fct_reorder(category, cv))

ggplot(cv_data, aes(x = cv, y = category, color = side)) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = 0, xend = cv, yend = category),
    linewidth = 0.6,
    alpha = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", cv)),
    hjust = -0.4,
    size = 3.2,
    color = pal$mid
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0.15,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = pal$light,
    linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(Batting = pal$bat, Pitching = pal$pit)) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title = "Category Competitiveness",
    subtitle = "Coefficient of variation: higher = more spread between teams",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  ) +
  h_grid

Three tiers emerge:

  1. Tight categories (CV < 0.10): AVG, WHIP, ERA — teams cluster together. Small edges matter enormously. A .003 improvement in team AVG gains a standings point. These are precision categories.

  2. Normal spread (CV 0.10-0.20): R, RBI, SO, HR, W — typical variation. These are the categories where drafting and roster management have clear, proportional returns.

  3. Wide-open categories (CV > 0.30): SB, SV — huge variance between teams. These are the categories where strategic allocation decisions (do I invest in speed? do I hoard closers?) create the biggest separation.

Category Deep Dives

Stolen Bases: A Category in Flux

Show code 
sb_stats <- map_dfr(PRIMARY_YEARS, function(yr) {
  vals <- standings |> filter(year == yr) |> pull(SB) |> na.omit()
  tibble(
    year = yr,
    mean = mean(vals),
    std = sd(vals),
    min = min(vals),
    max = max(vals)
  )
})

p_range <- ggplot(sb_stats, aes(x = year)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = min, ymax = max), fill = pal$bat, alpha = 0.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = mean), color = pal$bat, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(y = mean), color = pal$bat, size = 2.5) +
  geom_line(
    aes(y = min),
    color = pal$bat,
    linewidth = 0.3,
    alpha = 0.4,
    linetype = "dotted"
  ) +
  geom_line(
    aes(y = max),
    color = pal$bat,
    linewidth = 0.3,
    alpha = 0.4,
    linetype = "dotted"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = PRIMARY_YEARS) +
  labs(title = "SB range expanding", x = NULL, y = "Team stolen bases") +
  panel_title

p_sd <- ggplot(sb_stats, aes(x = year, y = std)) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(xend = year, y = 0, yend = std),
    color = pal$bat,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_point(color = pal$bat, size = 3.5) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = round(std)),
    vjust = -1.3,
    size = 3.5,
    color = pal$mid
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = PRIMARY_YEARS) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))) +
  labs(title = "SB variance doubling", x = NULL, y = "Standard deviation") +
  panel_title

p_range | p_sd

The stolen base landscape has transformed since 2019:

  • Standard deviation went from 21 to 49 — more than doubling
  • League mean jumped from ~97 to ~140
  • The top end exploded (117 → 240 peak) while the floor stayed relatively flat

The model uses time decay for SB (rate=0.90) because 2019’s SB dynamics look nothing like 2024-2025. The SGP denominator of 10.4 means each 10 steals gained is worth a standings point — but the spread is so wide that a team fully investing in speed can gain 10+ points in this single category.

Wins and Strikeouts: The Punt Categories

Show code 
punt_data <- standings |>
  filter(year %in% PRIMARY_YEARS) |>
  select(year, W, SO) |>
  pivot_longer(c(W, SO), names_to = "category", values_to = "value") |>
  drop_na(value) |>
  group_by(year, category) |>
  arrange(value) |>
  mutate(
    z = (value - mean(value)) / sd(value),
    rank = row_number(),
    is_punter = z < -1.5
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(year_fct = factor(year))

ggplot(punt_data, aes(x = rank, y = value, color = year_fct)) +
  geom_line(aes(group = year), linewidth = 0.4, alpha = 0.35) +
  geom_point(
    data = \(d) filter(d, !is_punter),
    size = 1.8,
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = \(d) filter(d, is_punter),
    size = 3.5,
    shape = 17,
    alpha = 0.9
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = year_pal, name = NULL) +
  facet_wrap(~category, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Punt Detection",
    subtitle = "Triangles mark teams with z-score < \u22121.5 — strategic category abandonment",
    x = "Rank (1 = worst)",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.key.size = unit(0.35, "cm"),
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = pal$bg, color = NA)
  )

The model enables punt detection for W and SO — the only two categories where it improved prediction accuracy. The evidence:

Wins punters:

  • 2021: Shoeless Joe’s (56 W, z=-1.82) and Shrooms (57 W, z=-1.72)
  • 2024: Ayal Feinberg (44 W, z=-2.38)
  • 2025: Gusteroids (52 W, z=-1.75)

Strikeouts punters:

  • 2022: Shoeless Joe’s (756 SO, z=-2.30)
  • 2023: Shrooms (1180 SO, z=-1.85)
  • 2024: Ayal Feinberg (1074 SO, z=-1.97)
  • 2025: Gusteroids (980 SO, z=-2.08)

These aren’t just bad teams — they’re teams making a strategic choice to sacrifice W and SO (likely running relief-heavy pitching staffs) to invest elsewhere. The model excludes them from the SGP calculation for those categories so the denominators reflect the competitive portion of the standings.

Strategy Note

The overlap between W and SO punters is near-total (same teams punt both). This is the classic “reliever-heavy” strategy — you sacrifice wins and strikeouts but gain ERA, WHIP, and saves. If you see multiple teams doing this, the remaining teams’ W and SO standings compress, making each marginal win or strikeout more valuable.

Saves: The Closer Market

Show code 
saves_data <- standings |>
  filter(year %in% PRIMARY_YEARS) |>
  select(year, SV) |>
  drop_na(SV) |>
  group_by(year) |>
  arrange(desc(SV)) |>
  mutate(rank = row_number()) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(year_fct = factor(year))

ggplot(saves_data, aes(x = rank, y = SV, color = year_fct, group = year)) +
  annotate(
    "rect",
    xmin = -Inf,
    xmax = Inf,
    ymin = 60,
    ymax = 100,
    fill = pal$good,
    alpha = 0.04
  ) +
  annotate(
    "rect",
    xmin = -Inf,
    xmax = Inf,
    ymin = 0,
    ymax = 25,
    fill = pal$pit,
    alpha = 0.04
  ) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = year_pal, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:10) +
  labs(
    title = "Save Distribution by Year",
    subtitle = "Sharp drop-offs between the haves and have-nots",
    x = "Team rank",
    y = "Saves"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = c(0.88, 0.82),
    legend.direction = "vertical",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.key.size = unit(0.35, "cm"),
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = alpha(pal$bg, 0.9), color = NA)
  )

The model uses robust regression for saves — the only category where this method won. Why? Because the saves distribution has extreme outlier behavior:

  • Teams with elite closers cluster at 60-97 saves
  • Teams without established closers plummet to 10-20
  • There’s often a cliff between the top 4-5 teams and the bottom half

Robust regression down-weights these extremes to estimate the “typical” cost of a standings point in the competitive middle. The denominator of 6.4 saves per standings point reflects the middle-of-the-pack reality, not the extremes.

The Composite Model: Why One Size Doesn’t Fit All

The sweep tested 320 configurations and found that different categories genuinely prefer different statistical treatments. The composite model uses 9 distinct configurations across 10 categories.

Composite Model Configuration
Each category's optimal statistical treatment
Category Method Supplemental Data Time Decay Punt Detection
Batting
R OLS – ● –
HR Pairwise Mean ● – –
RBI OLS ● – –
SB Pairwise Mean ● ● –
AVG Pairwise Mean – – –
Pitching
W Pairwise Median – ● ●
SV Robust Reg ● – –
SO Pairwise Mean – – ●
ERA OLS ● ● –
WHIP Pairwise Mean ● – –

What the configurations reveal

Categories that benefit from supplemental data (2015-2018): HR, RBI, SB, SV, ERA, WHIP. These categories existed in the 8-category era and their statistical structure hasn’t changed dramatically. More data = better estimates.

Categories that reject supplemental data: R, AVG, SO. R and SO didn’t exist before 2019 (obvious), and AVG’s tight distribution means the noise from the 8-category era data hurts more than the extra sample size helps.

Categories with time decay: R, SB, W, ERA. These are categories where recent seasons look different from older ones. The game is changing — more stolen bases, shifting pitching usage patterns, evolving ERA environments.

Punt detection categories: W, SO only. These are the only categories where teams make a clear strategic choice to abandon the category entirely. The punters are identifiable and removing them from the calibration data improves the model’s ability to predict the competitive portion of the standings.

Model Validation

Show code 
mean_rho <- mean(cv_df$rank_corr)

p_rank <- ggplot(cv_df, aes(x = factor(year), y = rank_corr)) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(xend = factor(year), y = 0.85, yend = rank_corr),
    color = pal$good,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_point(color = pal$good, size = 4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", rank_corr)),
    vjust = -1.3,
    size = 3.5,
    color = pal$mid,
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = mean_rho,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = pal$pit,
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 0.6,
    y = mean_rho + 0.006,
    label = sprintf("Mean = %.3f", mean_rho),
    color = pal$pit,
    size = 3,
    hjust = 0
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0.85, 1.02)) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  labs(
    title = "Rank correlation by held-out year",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Spearman \u03c1"
  ) +
  panel_title

nrmse_plot <- nrmse_df |>
  mutate(
    side = ifelse(category %in% BATTING, "Batting", "Pitching"),
    category = fct_reorder(category, nrmse)
  )

p_nrmse <- ggplot(nrmse_plot, aes(x = nrmse, y = category, color = side)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = nrmse, yend = category), linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", nrmse)),
    hjust = -0.4,
    size = 3.2,
    color = pal$mid
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = 0.25,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = pal$light,
    linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(Batting = pal$bat, Pitching = pal$pit)) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title = "Prediction error by category",
    x = "Normalized RMSE (lower = better)",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  panel_title +
  h_grid

p_rank | p_nrmse

The model validates well across all 6 held-out years:

  • Rank correlation: 0.91-0.99 per year (mean 0.961). This means the model’s denomination structure correctly predicts which teams will finish where in the standings.
  • Worst year: 2019 (0.91) — the oldest year in the primary window, which makes sense given the league has evolved since then.
  • Best categories: SV (nRMSE 0.13), R (0.18), ERA (0.18) — these denominators are highly predictable year over year.
  • Hardest categories: W (0.58), SB (0.45) — these have the most year-to-year volatility, consistent with the structural changes we see in the data.

Star Player Impact by Category

Show code 
team_ab <- 6514
team_ip <- 1226
repl_avg <- 0.245
repl_era <- 4.35
repl_whip <- 1.36
star_ab <- 550
star_ip <- 180

star_data <- tibble(
  category = c("R", "HR", "RBI", "SB", "AVG", "W", "SV", "SO", "ERA", "WHIP"),
  stat = c(100, 35, 100, 25, 0.290, 15, 35, 220, 3.20, 1.10),
  side = c(rep("Batting", 5), rep("Pitching", 5))
) |>
  left_join(DENOMS |> select(category, denom), by = "category") |>
  mutate(
    sgp = case_when(
      category == "AVG" ~ (stat - repl_avg) * (star_ab / team_ab) / denom,
      category == "ERA" ~ (repl_era - stat) * (star_ip / team_ip) / denom,
      category == "WHIP" ~ (repl_whip - stat) * (star_ip / team_ip) / denom,
      TRUE ~ stat / denom
    ),
    category = factor(category, levels = rev(c(BATTING, PITCHING)))
  )

h_total <- star_data |> filter(side == "Batting") |> pull(sgp) |> sum()
p_total <- star_data |> filter(side == "Pitching") |> pull(sgp) |> sum()

ggplot(star_data, aes(x = sgp, y = category, color = side)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = sgp, yend = category), linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.1f", sgp)),
    hjust = -0.4,
    size = 3.5,
    color = pal$mid,
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = max(star_data$sgp) * 0.8,
    y = 8.5,
    label = sprintf("Hitter total: %.1f SGP", h_total),
    color = pal$bat,
    size = 3.8,
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = max(star_data$sgp) * 0.8,
    y = 3.5,
    label = sprintf("Pitcher total: %.1f SGP", p_total),
    color = pal$pit,
    size = 3.8,
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(Batting = pal$bat, Pitching = pal$pit)) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.18))) +
  labs(
    title = "Star Player SGP by Category",
    subtitle = "A star pitcher generates more SGP per category, but across fewer roster slots",
    x = "SGP contribution",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  h_grid

A star-level hitter contributes SGP across 5 categories at roughly 2-3 SGP each. A star pitcher’s counting stats (W, SV, SO) generate 5-6 SGP per category — nearly double. However, a hitter’s rate stat contribution (AVG) is modest, while a pitcher’s rate stats (ERA, WHIP) involve team-level dilution that reduces their individual impact.

The Pitching Paradox

Individual star pitchers produce more raw SGP per category, but there are only 5 pitching categories vs. 5 batting categories, and hitters fill 15 roster slots vs. 11 for pitchers. The net effect: total league value is roughly 65% hitting / 35% pitching — yet your league has been increasing its pitching spend beyond this ratio.

Year-to-Year Stability

Show code 
year_denoms <- year_denoms_df |>
  mutate(
    side = ifelse(category %in% BATTING, "Batting", "Pitching"),
    category = factor(category, levels = c(BATTING, PITCHING))
  )

composites <- year_denoms |> distinct(category, composite, side)

ggplot(year_denoms, aes(x = year, y = denom, color = side)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    aes(ymin = composite * 0.75, ymax = composite * 1.25, fill = side),
    alpha = 0.06,
    color = NA
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    data = composites,
    aes(yintercept = composite),
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = pal$light,
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(Batting = pal$bat, Pitching = pal$pit)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(Batting = pal$bat, Pitching = pal$pit)) +
  facet_wrap(~category, scales = "free_y", ncol = 5) +
  labs(
    title = "Year-Level SGP Stability",
    subtitle = "Shaded band = \u00b125% of composite denominator",
    x = NULL,
    y = "SGP Denominator"
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = rel(0.65), angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.spacing = unit(0.8, "lines")
  )

Most categories stay within the 25% band year-over-year, which is expected with only 10 teams generating 9 gaps per year. The notable outliers:

  • W in 2024-2025: Denominator spiking — the gap between win totals is widening as more teams punt wins
  • SB in 2023-2024: Denominator increased with the stolen base explosion, but has stabilized in 2025
  • SO: Volatile year-over-year, partly because of the punt dynamic

Appendix: Data Sources & Methodology

Data: 6 primary calibration years (2019, 2021-2025) comprising 60 team-seasons across 10 categories. Supplemental data (2015-2018) used for 6 of 10 categories where it improves accuracy.

Validation: Leave-one-year-out cross-validation with Spearman rank correlation as the primary metric. Each held-out year trains on 5 years and predicts the 6th.

Composite model: 320 configurations tested across 4 SGP methods, 2 supplemental settings, 4 time-decay settings, and 2 punt-detection settings. Per-category winners assembled into a composite that outperforms any single global configuration (rank correlation 0.961 vs 0.955).

Team totals: Actual team AB and IP scraped from OnRoto (mean AB=6,514, mean IP=1,226 across primary years — notably lower than initial estimates of 7,300/1,300).
